content.full_text:
FPPC

Oct 20 9 15 AM '91

October 25, 1991

Mr. John Wallace esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: Phone conversation 10/24/91 and reference is made
Advice No.A-84-208 9/19/94

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Thank you for returning my call and discussing my role as an
elected Director for Santa Clara Valley Water District a
public agency for water utility and flood control.

There seems to be a road block (citing G.C. 1090) by the
agency to repair a leaking levee flowing from a public
project/lands on to private land which includes a National
Historical Building (1850 circa) owned by Mrs. Gross and
self. This excessive leakage is not only flowing onto our
property, but includes the lands of the United States of
America, Fish and Wildlife Service, which includes another
historical structure, housing a bird observatory office,
class rooms and facilities.

By Federal regulations, a National Historical Structure -
improvements and foundation are to be protected from water
damage and in this case, we cannot control it because it is
flowing from public lands.

I have repeated the problem to the District staff about this
problem and now, I am being told by District staff and the
general counsel, under Government Code 1090, the District may
in capable of repair because I would be in conflict of
interest of receiving improvements from the agency which I am
an elected official.

The subject levee and toe drain are on District lands and the
excess waters are flowing from their improvements, their fee
title on to other lands without providing protection.

As the elected representative from this area, I have been
asked by employees of the U.S. Government, members of the
community and the South Bay Yacht Club that portions of the
levee has some problems. Members of the yacht club told me
of a leak adjacent to their site (a few hundred feet away)
and at the street, the District staff told me it was the
yacht club's sprinkler system, but in reality, after
continued study and investigation, dedication of maintenance,
the District found it was a problem with a clogged toe drain
at that point causing the problem.

It was resolved by the District, but in my case, I am told

[illegible]

[Most of this document is too faint and blurry to transcribe accurately. The text appears to be a typed document with multiple paragraphs, but the content is largely illegible in this image quality. Individual words and sentences are too unclear to transcribe with confidence while maintaining accuracy.]

that it may be a conflict of interest if the District cures
my problem other than legal action against them.  I find
their attitude and decision under 1090 is incorrect.

If I understood you and as you stated in our phone
conversation, under Government regulation 18700.1, it does
not prohibit the District to make repairs on public
facilities that are causing damages to my property and I am
personally not in conflict with government codes that deal
with the actions of an elected official providing I abstain
from said action of the Board that deals with this case other
than as a private individual requesting service.

Very truly,

Robert W. Gross
Chairman of the Board
Santa Clara Valley Water District

cc:  U.S. Gov't., Fish and Wildlife Service
     SCVWD
Encl: Map of area

embedding.qa_text: October 25, 1991 Mr. John Wallace esq. Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 RE: Phone conversation 10/24/91 and reference is made Advice No.A-84-208 9/19/94 Dear Mr. Wallace: Thank you for returning my call and discussing my role as an elected Director for Santa Clara Valley Water District a public agency for water utility and flood control. There seems to be a road block (citing G.C. 1090) by the agency to repair a leaking levee flowing from a public project/lands on to private land which includes a National Historical Building (1850 circa) owned by Mrs. Gross and self. This excessive leakage is not only flowing onto our property, but includes the lands of the United States of America, Fish and Wildlife Service, which includes another historical structure, housing a bird observatory office, class rooms and facilities. By Federal regulations, a National Historical Structure - improvements and foundation are to be protected from water damage and in this case, we cannot control it because it is flowing from public lands. I have repeated the problem to the District staff about this problem and now, I am being told by District staff and the general counsel, under Government Code 1090, the District may in capable of repair because I would be in conflict of interest of receiving improvements from the agency which I am an elected official. The subject levee and toe drain are on District lands and the excess waters are flowing from their improvements, their fee title on to other lands without providing protection. As the elected representative from this area, I have been asked by employees of the U.S. Government, members of the community and the South Bay Yacht Club that portions of the levee has some problems. Members of the yacht club told me of a leak adjacent to their site (a few hundred feet away) and at the street, the District staff told me it was the yacht club's sprinkler system, but in reality, after continued study and investigation, dedication of maintenance, the District found it was a problem with a clogged toe drain at that point causing the problem. It was resolved by the District, but in my case, I am told The image provided is extremely faint and mostly illegible, with very little visible text that can be reliably transcribed or translated. Only a few scattered letters and numbers are partially visible, but the majority of the content cannot be reliably recovered due to the quality and density of the text. If you have another scan or a higher quality image, please provide it for better results. that it may be a conflict of interest if the District cures my problem other than legal action against them. I find their attitude and decision under 1090 is incorrect. If I understood you and as you stated in our phone conversation, under Government regulation 18700.1, it does not prohibit the District to make repairs on public facilities that are causing damages to my property
